preamble: The voiceless alveolar stop /t/ surfaces as the voiced alveolar flap [ɾ] in intervocalic position in North American English. This process, known as flapping or t-voicing, creates phonologically significant allophones that may yield homophonous pairs (e.g., metal/medal), though the underlying phonemic contrast is preserved in the mental grammar of speakers.

examples.note: Occurs between vowels in words like butter, letter, city, after sonorants (r, l, n)
Phonemically /bʌtɚ/ but phonetically realized with flap
Homophonous with ladder in many North American dialects
Systematic allophonic rule in Canadian English as in other North American varieties

context: Intervocalic position (between vowels), after sonorants in North American English
allophones_note: The underlying phoneme is /t/, which surfaces as [ɾ] in this environment while maintaining phonological distinctiveness from /d/ → [ɾ] homophony at the phonetic level

related.label: Underlying phonemic contrast lost in intervocalic flap output
